my_thoughts: The resolution works: relativity (cannot 'do' in general, but can control thoughts/inner actions), defines: initial doing = control of thoughts (minimal scope). The student is stuck on logical inconsistency (false paradox), which shows they understand: keep rules = 'doing', but cannot 'do' (paradox). They'll likely accept the resolution. Single-turn episode: paradox question → resolution → complete. Hypothesis: logical inconsistency resolutions lead to compliance in single-turn episodes—testable by tracking whether logical inconsistency resolutions end episodes.
accumulated_insight: T1: Student stuck on logical inconsistency ('We are told to keep rules. This presupposes we can keep them, which would be 'doing'. It seems to me incompatible with the idea that we cannot 'do'')—stuck on logical inconsistency via false paradox and conceptual inquiry. They understand: keep rules = 'doing', but cannot 'do' (paradox). The comparison_contrast mechanism resolves: relativity (cannot 'do' in general, but can control thoughts/inner actions), defines: initial doing = control of thoughts (minimal scope). Pattern: paradox question → resolution → [complete]. Low confidence because paradox resolutions can lead to compliance or further questions.
visible_response:
Everything is relative.
We can 'do' some inner actions, for we have
a certain control.
For instance, we have a certain control of our thoughts:
we can think about one thing or another.
This is the beginning of the possibility. If we
continue to keep our interests directed in a certain line, our thinking process acquires a
certain power and, after some time, it can create at least moments of self-awareness
which, when it comes more often and stays longer, can begin to change other things.
